1: hôšîʿâ YHWH kî gāmar ḥāsîd (hôšîʿâ YHWH kî gāmar ḥāsîd): 'Save, O LORD, for the godly man ceases.' The qal perfect gāmar ('to come to an end, cease') describes the ḥāsîd ('covenant-loyal one, godly person') as nearly extinct. The word ḥāsîd (from ḥesed, 'steadfast love') denotes one who embodies covenant faithfulness. Its near-disappearance signals covenantal crisis. The companion phrase kî passû ʾĕmûnîm mibbĕnê hāʾādām, 'for the faithful have vanished from among the children of man,' uses ʾĕmûnîm ('faithful ones') — those whose lives are characterized by ʾĕmûnâ (faithfulness/reliability).
2: šāwĕʾ yĕdabbĕrû ʾîš ʾet rēʿēhû (šāwĕʾ yĕdabbĕrû ʾîš ʾeṯ-rēʿēhû): 'Each speaks falsehood to his neighbor.' The noun šāwĕʾ ('vanity, falsehood, emptiness') describes speech that has no correspondence to reality. The phrase śĕpat ḥălāqôt bĕlēb wālēb yĕdabbĕrû, 'with flattering lips and a double heart they speak,' introduces bĕlēb wālēb ('with a double heart' — literally 'with heart and heart'). The doubled lēb signals divided loyalty: the speaker's external words do not match the internal reality. This linguistic duplicity is the social crisis the psalm laments.
3: yakrēt YHWH kol-śiptê ḥălāqôt (yaḵrēt YHWH kol-śipĕtê ḥălāqôṯ): 'May the LORD cut off all flattering lips.' The hiphil jussive yakrēt ('may he cut off') moves from description to petition. The hendiadys śiptê ḥălāqôt wĕlāšôn mĕdabberet gĕdōlôt, 'flattering lips and the tongue that speaks great things,' pairs the instrument (lips/tongue) with the content (great boasts/grand arrogance). The contrast between the godly who have ceased (v.1) and these boasters who dominate drives the prayer for reversal.
4: ʾăšer ʾāmĕrû lĕlāšōnēnû nagbîr (ʾăšer ʾāmĕrû lĕlāšōnēnû naḡḇîr): 'who have said, 'With our tongue we will prevail.'' The hiphil nagbîr ('we will be strong, prevail') — from gābar ('to be mighty') — claims that rhetorical power is sovereign. The phrase śĕpātênû ʾittānû, 'our lips are our own/with us,' asserts linguistic autonomy: speech is self-owned, accountable to no one. The closing question mî ʾādôn lānû, 'who is lord over us?', is the boast's logical conclusion and the psalm's central theological problem: the rejection of any authority beyond the self.
5: mišōd ʿăniyyîm mēʾanqat ʾebyônîm (mišōḏ ʿăniyyîm mēʾanqaṯ ʾeḇyônîm): 'Because of the devastation of the poor, because of the groaning of the needy.' The noun šōd ('devastation, destruction') describes violent oppression; ʾanqâ ('groaning') is the sound of extremity. YHWH's response is direct speech: ʿattâ ʾāqûm yōʾmar YHWH, 'now I will arise, says the LORD.' The divine yōʾmar ('says the LORD') — an oracle formula — gives the next words prophetic authority: ʾāšît bĕyēšaʿ yāpîaḥ lô, 'I will place in safety the one who pants/longs for it.' The verb yāpîaḥ ('to breathe heavily, pant') captures the desperate longing of the oppressed.
6: ʾimrôt YHWH ʾimrôt tĕhōrôt (ʾimrôt YHWH ʾimrôt ṭĕhōrôṯ): 'The words of the LORD are pure words.' The repetition ʾimrôt ... ʾimrôt ('words ... words') is emphatic: this is definitional. The adjective tāhôr ('pure, clean') is the technical term for cultic purity — what is unadulterated, without contamination. The simile kesep ṣārûp bĕʿalîl lāʾāreṣ mĕzuqqāq šibʿātayim, 'silver refined in a furnace on the ground, purified seven times,' specifies the purity: smelted and resmelted until all dross is gone. In context this is a pointed contrast to the hollow words of v.2-4 — human speech is śāwĕʾ ('vain'); divine speech is tāhôr.
7: ʾattâ YHWH tišmĕrēm (ʾattâ YHWH tišmĕrēm): 'You, O LORD, will keep them.' The subject ʾattâ ('you') is emphatic — against all human protectors who have failed, YHWH is the one who guards. The referent of tišmĕrēm is debated: the 'them' may refer to 'the poor' of v.5 or 'the words' of v.6. Both yield coherent sense. The phrase tiṣṣĕrennû min-haddôr zû lĕʿôlām, 'you will guard us from this generation forever,' shifts to first person — the psalmist includes himself and the community among those preserved. The phrase min-haddôr zû ('from this generation') indicates the corrupt contemporaries of vv.1-4.
8: sābîb rĕšāʿîm yithallākûn (sāḇîḇ rĕšāʿîm yiṯhallāḵûn): 'The wicked walk about on every side.' The hitpael yithallākûn ('walk back and forth') suggests restless movement of the wicked who dominate the landscape. The phrase kĕrum zullût libnê ʾādām, 'when what is vile is exalted among the children of man,' names the inversion: zullût ('vileness, baseness') rises to prominence. The psalm ends not with resolution but with honest observation — the present moment is bleak. Faith in YHWH's word (v.6) and his guardianship (v.7) does not eliminate the experience of living in a corrupt generation.
